summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is a 'rational equation'?

A rational equation contains at least one expression with a variable in the denominator, such as 1/x + 2 = 5.

What is an 'extraneous solution'?

An extraneous solution arises when we multiply both sides by an expression containing the variable, potentially introducing solutions that make the original denominator zero.

What is a 'proportion'?

A proportion is an equation of the form a/b = c/d, stating that two ratios are equal.

What is the 'LCD method' for solving rational equations?

The LCD method eliminates all fractions by multiplying each term by the least common denominator, yielding a simpler polynomial equation.

What is a 'work-rate problem'?

Work-rate problems model situations where multiple agents work together, using 1/time for each rate and summing to find the combined rate.

A boat travels 30 miles upstream (against current c = 2 mph) and 30 miles downstream. If the boat speed in still water is 8 mph, what is the total travel time?

Upstream speed = 8 - 2 = 6 mph, time = 30/6 = 5 hours. Downstream speed = 8 + 2 = 10 mph, time = 30/10 = 3 hours. Total = 5 + 3 = 8 hours.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: What does 'cross-multiplication' mean in solving rational equations?

Cross-multiplication is valid when two fractions are set equal. It eliminates both denominators at once: a/b = c/d becomes ad = bc.
